text: The surgeon performs a total laparoscopic myomectomy on a patient with a large 15 cm myoma involving the left round ligament. The procedure begins with an adnexectomy, cutting the round ligament, left fallopian tube, and utero-ovarian ligament to improve visibility of the myoma. Bipolar coagulation is used to safely cut tissue, aiming to minimize bleeding and enhance the view of the operative field.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament, revealing a large vein surrounding the myoma. The dissection progresses toward the bladder, providing a clear view of the myoma. The surgeon uses a bipolar device to control the large vein and reduce the risk of hemorrhage, continuing the peritoneal section with careful attention to the vein.

The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation and cutting techniques to dissect the myometrial capsule surrounding a large myoma, applying traction and counter-traction to control the large veins. The posterior leaf of the broad ligament is opened to increase the myoma's mobility. The surgeon carefully dissects the capsule anteriorly, aiming to control over fifty percent of the capsule before extracting the myoma. A 10mm laparoscopic forceps is used to medially tract the myoma near the external iliac vessel, facilitating its removal from the uterine bed.

The surgeon completes the extraction of a large myoma and performs hemostasis on the left uterine artery. The medial insertion of the myoma is cut to improve visibility of the residual uterus. Due to the uterine manipulator being found in the myoma bed, a supracervical hysterectomy is initiated. The round ligament, tube, and utero-ovarian ligament are cut, followed by the left lateral aspect of the uterine isthmus. The right uterine artery is controlled and cut, and the supracervical hysterectomy is progressively performed for better control of the radial cervix.

The surgeon completes the supracervical hysterectomy, leaving only the posterior part of the uterus cut. The residual cervix is clearly visible, allowing for a safe trachelectomy. The large volume of the uterus and myoma previously complicated the bladder dissection, which can now be performed safely.

The surgeon pushes the bladder down and performs a classical colpotomy using a monopolar hook to remove the residual cervix. The left, posterior, and right aspects of the vagina are cut. Due to the large size of the uterus and myoma, an intra-abdominal hemisection is performed using a chardonnay knife for morselation, facilitating vaginal extraction of uterine and myoma pieces. The fifteen millimeter myoma is cut into smaller pieces and extracted vaginally using a ten millimeter forceps.

The surgeon identifies and controls the left ureter, ensuring its normal rotation is visible. The vaginal closure begins using a Vicryl 0 suture, incorporating the previous myoma bed into the closure. The surgeon employs the Roeder knot, an extracorporeal knotting technique, starting from the lateral aspect of the vagina. The entire mucosa, including the posterior mucosa which appears deeper due to the myoma location, is included in the sutures. The closure of the vaginal cuff is completed with four or five stitches, ensuring the entire mucosa is secured.
The surgeon completes the final knot using an intracorporeal knotting technique. Due to the large size of the left myoma, a left adnexal pexy is performed to reduce the risk of ovarian torsion.
